title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Mason County township mulls pausing data centers for a year over power, water and noise concerns.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Monday morning, July 13, and the big story today is a proposed one-year moratorium on data centers in Pere Marquette Township in Mason County.
That sounds like another local board hitting the brakes. Why now, and what does it mean for the rest of the state?

Trustees there will review the pause citing energy demands, water use, and noise impacts before any projects arrive. They're also looking at a $1.36 million park entrance contract at the same meeting.
So the moratorium is the headline item, but it's bundled with everyday township business. That tells me residents are pushing back hard on these big facilities.
Exactly. This fits a pattern we've seen building across Michigan. Several townships and counties have already enacted or are considering similar pauses to study the impacts first.
Walk me through the bigger picture. How did we get here with all these moratoriums popping up?

It started with growing interest from hyperscale developers looking for sites with cheap power and water access. Rural areas in western and northern Michigan became targets, and locals quickly raised flags about strain on the grid, wells, and quiet farmland.
What kind of strain are we talking about for regular folks?
A single large data center can pull as much power as a small city. Without new generation coming online fast enough, that means tighter supply and potential rate hikes for everyone else.
We saw that play out yesterday when residents in western Michigan blocked Consumers Energy's 1.4 gigawatt gas plant. The utility pulled the whole proposal.
Right. That plant was eyed to serve big users like data centers. Now ratepayers could face higher costs as demand keeps climbing.
And water is another flashpoint, right? These facilities need massive cooling.
Yes. Townships like Bay County just froze new proposals to study power and water supplies. Garfield Township planners in Grand Traverse County advanced their own moratorium option over the weekend.
So Pere Marquette is the latest in a wave. How many places have hit pause already?
At least a handful statewide, from Bay County to Ingham County where Lansing is holding a hearing today on a six-month pause after a $120 million project was withdrawn.
And Green Oak Township in Livingston County just approved new zoning rules to control where these high-resource facilities can go.
That gives local boards clearer tools. Residents there now have guardrails instead of reacting after the fact.
What about the tax side? I keep hearing about hidden incentives and fights over property values.

Consumers Energy warned yesterday that disputed taxes on data center projects could get passed to customers this winter. Separately, secret tax breaks already cost the state $13 million according to reports.
That money would have gone to local services. So taxpayers are subsidizing these projects without even knowing which ones.
State lawmakers are eyeing new guardrails on power use and incentives that would apply statewide. Passage would shift some oversight away from pure local control.
So if Pere Marquette passes the one-year pause today, what happens next for that township?
It buys time to write rules on noise, water, and grid connections. Similar to what Dowagiac residents will get their first look at during a public forum there tonight.
Dowagiac is hosting a forum on potential developments. Locals can ask about noise, water, and electric strain before anything formal moves forward.
Exactly. These meetings are where the real pushback is happening.
And with multiple boards voting today, it feels like a coordinated moment of caution across the state.
It does. The pattern is clear: communities want to study impacts before approving anything that could reshape their power bills and water supplies for decades.
What should listeners watch for in the coming weeks?
Whether these moratoriums stick, how the legislative bills progress, and if Consumers or other utilities bring new proposals after the recent setbacks.
And whether the tax dispute gets resolved without hitting households.
All of that is on the table right now.
Let's shift to quick hits. Green Oak Township just passed those new zoning rules. How does that change things for Livingston County?
The overlay district gives the board tools to regulate high-electricity and water users. It controls where large data centers can locate going forward.
Residents there now have clearer guardrails instead of waiting for proposals to land.
Next up, Dowagiac is holding its public forum tonight. Locals get to voice questions on noise, water, and grid strain before any formal plans advance.
That's a chance for direct input early in the process.
Yesterday's rural push that killed the Consumers 1.4 GW plant is still rippling. Without that power, supply tightens for everyone as AI demand grows.
Ratepayers could see higher costs sooner rather than later.
State lawmakers are also looking at bills for stricter power rules and tax incentives. Those would affect every township with proposals on the table.
Lansing is holding its own hearing today on a six-month pause after that $120 million project withdrawal.
And Consumers warned rates could rise if the tax fight drags on. They're paying millions in disputed taxes that might get passed along.
Bay County already froze all new proposals to study power and water impacts during peak summer demand.
Garfield Township planners backed a freeze option over the weekend while residents debate land use and power strain in northwest Michigan.
And secret tax breaks have already cost the state $13 million, draining funds from local services.
Those are the quick hits for today.
Now for on deck. What meetings can listeners actually attend today?
Several key ones are happening right now. Lenox Township Board of Trustees has the first reading of its I-94 Development District ordinance and data center regulations.
Pere Marquette Charter Township Board is voting on that one-year moratorium plus the $1.36 million park project.
Lansing City Council has a public hearing on a temporary moratorium, and its Development & Planning committee is also taking up the 182-day pause.
Solon Township Board is considering an extension of its data center moratorium and related ordinances.
Those are the actionable meetings for anyone who wants to show up or weigh in.
Plenty happening across the state today.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Pere Marquette Weighs Year-Long Data Center Pause as Energy and Water Worries Grow
